eras.name: Joseon Dynasty Hanbok
eras.description: Over five centuries of Confucian rule, the hanbok evolved into a refined system of dress encoding social rank, age, and occasion. The jeogori jacket and chima skirt silhouette became iconic, with colours and fabrics strictly regulated by sumptuary laws.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Joseon Dynasty court lady wearing a flowing silk hanbok with a short jade-green jeogori and a voluminous crimson chima skirt, embroidered with golden phoenixes, standing in a traditional wooden hanok courtyard with curved tile roofs, soft golden hour lighting.

eras.name: The Modern Girl (Singnyeoseong)
eras.description: During Japanese colonial rule, urban Korean women began adopting Western dress alongside the hanbok, creating a hybrid aesthetic. The 'Modern Girl' or singnyeoseong became a symbol of modernity and cultural tension, blending bobbed hair and Western silhouettes with Korean identity.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1930s Seoul street scene showing a young Korean woman in a hybrid outfit of a shortened silk hanbok top with a Western pleated skirt and cloche hat, walking past Japanese-era storefronts, sepia-toned vintage photography style with soft grain.

eras.name: Post-War Textile Boom
eras.description: South Korea's rapid industrialisation turned the nation into a major textile exporter. Korean designers began adapting Western patterns for local production, and nylon and synthetic fabrics became status symbols in a society rebuilding from the devastation of war.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1960s Korean textile factory scene transitioning to a fashion showroom, young women in neat A-line skirts and nylon blouses examining fabric bolts, Seoul cityscape visible through large windows, documentary photography style with warm tungsten tones.

eras.name: Dongdaemun & Street Fashion
eras.description: Seoul's Dongdaemun Market became the world's fastest fashion supply chain, producing designs from sketch to rack in three days. Korean street fashion emerged as a distinct aesthetic, mixing oversized silhouettes with meticulous grooming and layering.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A late-night Dongdaemun Market scene with neon-lit fabric stalls and young Korean shoppers in oversized layered streetwear, wide-leg trousers and chunky sneakers, stacks of colourful fabric rolls in the background, cinematic neon-noir lighting.

eras.name: Hallyu & K-Fashion Wave
eras.description: The global Korean Wave propelled K-fashion into mainstream consciousness. K-pop idols became fashion icons, Korean beauty standards influenced global aesthetics, and Seoul designers gained recognition at international fashion weeks for their blend of minimalism, maximalism, and technical innovation.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Seoul Gangnam district scene featuring a young Korean fashion influencer in a structured oversized blazer with exaggerated shoulders, layered over a pastel mini skirt and chunky white sneakers, neon Hangul signage reflecting off wet pavement, cinematic K-drama lighting.

eras.name: Neo-Hanbok & Digital Fashion
eras.description: A new generation of designers reimagined the hanbok for contemporary wear, fusing traditional construction with modern fabrics and streetwear sensibilities. Simultaneously, Korea became a leader in digital fashion, virtual garments, and metaverse-ready clothing design.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A futuristic Seoul fashion show combining traditional and digital, a model wearing a neo-hanbok with holographic organza jeogori over modern black trousers, augmented reality patterns floating around the garment, minimalist white runway with LED floor projections.

textiles.name: Mosi (Ramie Cloth)
textiles.description: UNESCO-recognised Hansan mosi is an ultra-fine ramie cloth prized for its cool, crisp texture and translucency, traditionally used for summer hanbok garments.
textiles.technique: Hand-weaving of hand-split ramie fibres on a traditional back-strap loom

textiles.name: Gapsa Silk Gauze
textiles.description: A delicate, semi-transparent silk fabric used for the finest court hanbok, requiring exceptional skill to produce its even, gossamer-thin weave.
textiles.technique: Plain weave using highly twisted raw silk threads with controlled tension

textiles.name: Nubi (Quilted Fabric)
textiles.description: Traditional Korean quilting technique creating densely stitched parallel lines on silk or cotton, used for winter clothing and accessories with natural cotton padding.
textiles.technique: Even parallel running stitches at 3-5mm intervals through layered fabric and cotton batting

textiles.name: Sambe (Hemp Cloth)
textiles.description: Sturdy hemp textile historically worn by commoners and used for mourning dress, valued for its durability and natural antimicrobial properties.
textiles.technique: Hand-spun hemp fibres woven on a floor loom, then bleached and pounded for softness

climate.fashionImplication: Korea's four distinct seasons with hot, humid summers and cold, dry winters drive strong seasonal wardrobe rotation and layering culture, historically inspiring lightweight ramie and silk for summer and padded quilted garments for winter.

culturalInfluences: Confucian social hierarchy and dress codes
K-pop and K-drama global cultural exports
Korean beauty (K-beauty) standards and skincare-fashion intersection
Rapid post-war industrialisation and economic miracle
Buddhist temple aesthetics and natural dyeing traditions
Military service culture and its impact on menswear
PC bang and internet cafe culture shaping digital fashion

emergingTrends: Sustainable Korean hemp and ramie revival
Tech-integrated smart fabrics from Korean textile giants
